hard.next_step: Follow up on lunch offer for 7th/8th July in Dallas or call week of 29 June, post-cancellation of the iSupplier demo, to check timing for the Ariba replacement; qualify whether the AI vendor-management need is one-off or part of the full S2P suite replacement, and multi-thread above Lauren Flores.

north_star_verdict.headline: Single-threaded through Product Director Lauren Flores for 6+ months with no economic buyer or decision maker reached; the 17 Jun supplier demo was deprioritized over the full S2P replacement, and recent touches are all rep-side lunch/meeting invites awaiting a reply. The deal is alive but the buyer has gone quiet and the scope decision is stalled.
north_star_verdict.math: Created 10 Dec 2025 (208 days ago); 207 days to the 29 Jan 2027 close. Still in Qualified since 24 Jun with no Formal_Eval or Shortlisted submission date recorded, so time-in-structured-evaluation is zero. A full S2P Ariba replacement at Goldman typically runs 12-15 months; to close 29 Jan 2027 the buyer must reach Vendor Selected by roughly Oct 2026 to leave time for negotiation, InfoSec and contracting. Current pace (still qualifying scope, no EB engaged, demo deprioritized 17 Jun) will not hit the date. LastActivityDate 2 Jul is rep outreach, not buyer inbound; a 24 Jun health note flags 'no inbound in last 14 days'.
evidence: Stage Qualified since 24 Jun with no progression to Formal Evaluation or Shortlisted (no submission dates recorded)
17 Jun: iSupplier/Agentic supplier demo cancelled and deprioritized over the full S2P replacement per Lauren (Task, 17 Jun)
Scope ambiguity (one-off vendor-management module vs full suite replacement) open since 4 Mar and still unresolved (Task, 26 Jun and 2 Jul)
Single contact Lauren Flores (Product Director) across 6+ months; Jason Savarese (Partner, EB), Cade Flitter (VP, DM) and Ken Litton (MD) never engaged
2 Jul to-do: 'Move above Lauren as soon as possible to Cade Flitter and Jason [Savarese]; Ken Litton has moved to the CORE divisional-operations role'
No quantified ROI/TCO vs SAP Ariba; buyer stated only qualitative pain ('fragmented supplier master preventing onboarding', email 12 Mar)
Recent activity (24 Jun-2 Jul) is rep-side lunch and meet-up outreach awaiting Lauren's reply, not buyer engagement

metrics.narrative: Lauren Flores articulated a clear qualitative pain in her 12 Mar email: 'I'm looking for an AI native supplier master that will integrate with our ecosystem. Currently we have a more fragmented supplier master preventing activities like onboarding.' That establishes the operational problem (fragmented supplier master, blocked onboarding) but no quantified impact has been captured: no supplier-onboarding cycle-time, no headcount or error-rate figure, and no TCO comparison versus the incumbent SAP Ariba. Arthur shared a budgetary sizing on 6 Apr ('10 power users and 50 business users, 9,000 suppliers and 250 critical'), but that is Zycus's pricing envelope, not a buyer-owned value case. Quantifying onboarding and AI-negotiation savings with Lauren, then carrying that number to the economic buyer, is the metric gap to close.

economic_buyer.narrative: The economic buyer is identifiable by title but has never been engaged. Jason Savarese (Partner: Global Division Head, Corporate Planning and Management) sits above the deal owner Lauren Flores in the same CPM division and carries the budget authority for a $500k S2P investment. The 2 Jul internal to-do makes this explicit: 'Move above Lauren as soon as possible to Cade Flitter and Jason [Savarese]; Ken Litton has moved to a divisional operations role as head of CORE.' No email, call or meeting shows Jason engaged, and Ken Litton (MD, CPM) who was a secondary budget candidate has just moved out of the sourcing remit. Budget authority is therefore mapped on the org chart but wholly unactivated, which is the central early-stage risk. Securing a Lauren-led introduction to Jason Savarese is the move.

decision_criteria.narrative: No formal evaluation criteria have been documented, which is consistent with a deal still in Qualified with no RFI/RFP round run. The one criterion the buyer has voiced is directional: Lauren wants an 'AI native supplier master' that 'will integrate with our ecosystem' (email 12 Mar), so ecosystem integration and AI-native supplier onboarding are inferred priorities, not confirmed scorecard items. Whether the evaluation is a one-off vendor-management module or a full Ariba replacement is itself unresolved (open since 4 Mar), so the criteria set cannot yet be fixed. Running a scoping session with Lauren to capture the business-requirement list and the integration touchpoints is the step that would convert this from gap to confirmed.

decision_process.narrative: The buyer's decision process is not yet visible because the deal has not entered a structured evaluation. What the activity sequence shows is a single-threaded qualification: every touch since December has run through Lauren Flores, and the two attempts to formalize (the 19 May iSupplier demo and the 17 Jun Agentic supplier demo) were pushed and then deprioritized. No approval chain, no procurement or InfoSec entry point, and no buying-committee cadence has surfaced. At Goldman Sachs an S2P suite replacement will require division sign-off (Jason Savarese) plus software-sourcing ownership (Cade Flitter), neither of whom is in the process yet. Mapping who must approve and in what order is the process gap.

paper_process.narrative: Paper process is genuinely unknown, which is expected at Qualified. No MSA, Order Form, SOW, legal, procurement channel or InfoSec review has been discussed, and no current-contract-expiration date for the incumbent SAP Ariba has been captured as a forcing function. At a regulated BFSI account like Goldman, the eventual gates (InfoSec/SOC review, DPA, vendor onboarding through their supplier portal, and legal redlines) will be heavyweight, so surfacing the Ariba renewal/expiration date and the procurement route early would create the only credible forcing function for the 29 Jan 2027 date. Until scope and an evaluation are agreed, there is no paper motion to track.

identify_pain.narrative: The pain is confirmed and owned by Lauren Flores (Product Director, Source to Pay): 'Currently we have a more fragmented supplier master preventing activities like onboarding' and a stated desire for an 'AI native supplier master that will integrate with our ecosystem' (email 12 Mar). The 17 Jun note shows this pain widening from a one-off vendor-management need toward a full S2P replacement of SAP Ariba, but that expansion is still being qualified rather than confirmed. The gap is depth and reach: the pain is articulated by one Product Director, not yet by the software-sourcing owner (Cade Flitter) or the division head (Jason Savarese), and it is qualitative with no cost or cycle-time attached. Broadening and quantifying the pain with senior stakeholders is the move.

champion.narrative: Lauren Flores (Product Director, Source to Pay, CPM) is a developing champion, not yet a proven one. What has developed: she initiated the AI supplier-master conversation (12 Mar), scheduled demos, registered for Pinnacle, engaged on a personal level (Houston Rodeo invite, personal-email touches), and Arthur has kept a warm Jan-Jul cadence. What is still missing is the decisive part of a champion: she has not introduced Jason Savarese or Cade Flitter, has not driven an internal evaluation, and let the 17 Jun demo be deprioritized without pushing back. She could not attend events for Goldman compliance/recording reasons, which limits informal access. Her advocacy is real but untested at the power level, and with no second relationship Zycus is exposed if she reprioritizes; building her into a sponsor who opens the door upward is the priority.

competition.narrative: This is a displacement play against incumbent SAP Ariba: the entire thread is framed as an 'Ariba replacement' (Next Step, 17 Jun), so Ariba's integration depth and switching inertia are the primary threat, not any active challenger. Coupa, Spend HQ and Sievo are logged in Competitors__c but carry no buyer-stated preference or recent evaluation signal, so they read as background alternatives rather than live rivals. The real contest is Zycus versus status-quo Ariba. The wedge is exactly what Lauren asked for: an AI-native supplier master and Agentic/Merlin supplier onboarding that Ariba's static workflow cannot match, backed by a quantified onboarding and negotiation ROI. That narrative has not yet been landed in front of the buyer, and the demo that would carry it was deprioritized on 17 Jun.

deal_movement.summary: Activity is high but rep-sided. Stage advanced Initial Interest to Qualified (24 Jun) and amount doubled 250k to 500k (2 Feb), but the two demo attempts stalled: the 19 May iSupplier demo slipped and the 17 Jun Agentic supplier demo was deprioritized over the full S2P replacement. The last genuine buyer two-way exchange was Lauren's April emails scheduling the (later cancelled) demo; since mid-June the trail is Arthur chasing lunch and meet-up invites (24 Jun, 2 Jul) with no buyer reply, and a 24 Jun health note flags no inbound in 14 days.

items.change: Stage moved Initial Interest to Qualified
Close date pushed 31 Dec 2026 to 29 Jan 2027
iSupplier/Agentic supplier demo cancelled and deprioritized over full S2P replacement (per Lauren)
Amount raised 250k to 500k as scope broadened toward full suite
Ken Litton moved to the CORE divisional-operations role, removing a secondary budget contact

competitive_position.summary: The single strongest current threat is the incumbent, SAP Ariba: this is explicitly framed as an 'Ariba replacement' (17 Jun), so status-quo inertia and Ariba's embedded integration are what Zycus must overcome, not an active challenger. Coupa, Spend HQ and Sievo are logged in Competitors__c but show no buyer preference or recent evaluation, so they sit dormant behind Ariba. We win by activating the AI-native wedge Lauren already asked for (Agentic/Merlin supplier onboarding and an AI supplier master) that Ariba's static workflow cannot match, and pairing it with a quantified onboarding/negotiation ROI carried to the economic buyer.

best_practice_check.summary: Three win-strategy levers matter most now: break the single thread up to power, land the AI wedge against Ariba, and pin the scope so a formal evaluation can start.
flags: Multi-thread above Lauren to Cade Flitter (VP, software sourcing owner) and Jason Savarese (Partner, budget owner); single-threaded for 6+ months is the top risk.
Activate the Merlin/Agentic AI supplier-onboarding wedge Lauren asked for on 12 Mar; it is the only differentiator against incumbent Ariba's inertia.
Resolve the one-off-vs-full-suite scope question (open since 4 Mar) to fix deal size and unlock a formal evaluation.

deal_scores_evidence.summary: Warm but single-threaded Ariba-replacement play at Goldman Sachs: Lauren Flores (Product Director) is the only engaged contact after 6+ months, the economic buyer and decision maker have never been reached, and the demo that would carry the AI wedge was deprioritized on 17 Jun.

win_position.text: Early and single-threaded: Lauren Flores wants an AI-native supplier master to replace SAP Ariba (email, 12 Mar) and stayed personally engaged Jan-Jun, but no economic buyer or decision maker has been reached and the deal favors incumbent inertia.
Jason Savarese (Partner, budget owner) and Cade Flitter (VP, software sourcing) have never engaged, and the 2 Jul to-do is still just to move above Lauren for the first time.
SAP Ariba is the entrenched incumbent and no Merlin/Agentic AI wedge or quantified TCO has landed to justify a switch; the demo that would carry it was deprioritized on 17 Jun.

deal_momentum.text: Momentum stalled: the 17 Jun iSupplier/Agentic supplier demo was cancelled and deprioritized over the full S2P replacement (Task, 17 Jun).
No buyer inbound in the last two weeks; recent activity is rep-side lunch and meet-up outreach awaiting Lauren's reply (Tasks, 24 Jun-2 Jul).
Direction of travel is up, not down: amount doubled 250k to 500k (2 Feb) and Lauren is steering from a point module toward a full Ariba replacement.

customer_commitment.text: Commitment is soft: Lauren engages warmly (Pinnacle registration, demo scheduling) but has not introduced senior stakeholders or committed to a formal evaluation timeline.
The scope decision (one-off vendor management vs full suite) has been open since 4 Mar and blocks any formal evaluation from starting.

deal_risk.text: Economic buyer Jason Savarese and decision maker Cade Flitter are unmapped and unengaged; with Ken Litton moved to the CORE ops role (2 Jul), Zycus has only one relationship.
No quantified business case for the $500k spend; the buyer articulated qualitative pain only ('fragmented supplier master preventing onboarding', 12 Mar).

critical_signals.text: The 17 June iSupplier and Agentic supplier demo was cancelled after Lauren deprioritized it in favor of the full S2P replacement, so the momentum step stalled.
Ken Litton moved to the CORE divisional-operations role on 2 July, so the path to budget now runs through Jason Savarese (Partner) and Cade Flitter (VP), neither of whom has ever engaged.
This is a straight displacement of incumbent SAP Ariba, so the real threat is switching inertia rather than any active challenger, and the AI wedge to beat it has not yet been shown.
The deal is sized at $500k with no quantified ROI or TCO versus Ariba and the budget owner not engaged, so the price is not yet justified.
